chunk_summary: Catch-up feeding after acute illness in children 6–24 months focuses on restoring growth and appetite through planned meals, energy-dense foods, and monitoring progress over two weeks.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of catch-up feeding after acute illness in children aged 6–24 months.
Learn how to create a 2-week catch-up feeding plan with specific meal and snack guidelines.
Recognize the role of breastfeeding and energy-dense foods in post-illness recovery.
Identify when to escalate care if catch-up feeding fails or symptoms persist.
key_facts: Post-illness catch-up feeding should include 3 meals, 2 snacks, and 1 extra 'minimeal' daily for 14 days.
Meals should include a staple, protein, fat, and an iron-rich item, with fruits/vegetables most days.
Avoid tea/juice and limit meals to ≤30 minutes; offer water between meals.
Recheck weight in 2 weeks or earlier if intake deteriorates.
Persistent growth faltering or symptoms like vomiting, diarrhea, or fever warrant further evaluation and possible referral.
reference_quote: During illness, continue breastfeeding and offer small, soft, energy-dense foods as tolerated rather than restricting to watery liquids. After recovery, children should be offered food more often than usual and encouraged to eat more. — WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding
